title: ASIC ‘asleep at the wheel’ over junkets
full_text:
Former Nationals senator John “Wacka” Williams, who spent much of the past decade waging a campaign against misconduct in banking and financial services, fears ASIC is asleep at the wheel following revelations about The Inside Network’s promotion of perks for planners.
“It looks like ASIC is not doing its job once again,” a now retired Mr Williams told The Australian on Monday. “If there are shonks out there in the corporate world, ASIC is the corporate cop, do your job. You’ve got the resources, you’ve got the money, do your job.” Last week, The Australian revealed that a decade after Canberra moved to curb conflicts in financial advice, the culture of hospitality has staged a comeback. The old playbook of overseas trips, lavish dinners and long afternoons at the races, or the golf course, was meant to be consigned to history after the Future of Financial Advice reforms.
The reforms included a crackdown on conflicted remuneration structures, including commissions and volume-based payments. There was also a duty for financial advisers to act in the best interests of their clients, subject to a ‘reasonable steps’ qualification, and place the interests of their clients ahead of their own.
At the centre of this is events, education and networking group The Inside Network, which has built a business connecting fund managers with advisers.
The corporate matchmaker has been enlisted by Australian Ethical, Cooper Investors, Alphinity Investment Management, Martin Currie, Charter Hall, MLC Asset Management and Perennial Partners.
The sums involved are high. For between $15,000 and $60,000, fund managers can secure speaking spots at events where advisers are gathered to hear about alternatives, equities, fixed income and the economic outlook. Colonial First State has paid $1m over three years to be a foundation partner.
“It is most concerning what you have reported,” Mr Williams said of revelations about The Inside Network. “Surely ASIC is responsible if there is wrongdoing in this field and should take a very close look at this issue.” In response to a request for comment ASIC said via email: “ASIC does not generally comment on our regulatory activities.” Inside Network sits within a broader group that also includes advice firm Wattle Partners, asset consultant Atchison and public relations business Capital Outcomes. The businesses are owned by financial advisers Jamie Nemtsas and Drew Meredith.
Inside Network is not alone in this profitable corner. But few appear to operate through a structure as intricate as that linking Inside Network and its related entities. Last month, The Inside Network hosted an “inner circle” dinner at Melbourne restaurant Society. It has staged events and conferences in Cape Schanck and Noosa Heads. The schedule moves to Queenstown in August.
In his farewell speech to parliament, Mr Williams recalled how meeting the victims of Storm Financial galvanised his push for law reform. “It looked like they would be kicked out their houses onto the street. They were way past their working life. They could not rebuild. All I could promise them was a parliamentary inquiry, which we had,” he said. “The government’s response to that inquiry became the FOFA laws.”
